abstract: Spoken dialogue modeling poses challenges beyond text-based language modeling, requiring real-time interaction, turn-taking, and backchanneling. While most Spoken Dialogue Models (SDMs) operate in half-duplex mode-processing one turn at a time - emerging full-duplex SDMs can listen and speak simultaneously, enabling more natural conversations. However, current evaluations remain limited, focusing mainly on turn-based metrics or coarse corpus-level analyses. To address this, we introduce Full-Duplex-Bench, a benchmark that systematically evaluates key interactive behaviors: pause handling, backchanneling, turn-taking, and interruption management. Our framework uses automatic metrics for consistent, reproducible assessment and provides a fair, fast evaluation setup. By releasing our benchmark and code, we aim to advance spoken dialogue modeling and foster the development of more natural and engaging SDMs.

keyInsights: Commercial full-duplex systems (e.g., Gemini Live) and cascaded architectures with explicit control modules (e.g., Freeze-Omni) generally exhibit superior control over interactive behaviors.
End-to-end full-duplex models often struggle with discerning user pauses from turn-yielding signals, generating human-like backchannels, and maintaining semantic coherence during interruptions.

There is an observed trade-off between response speed and the ability to manage complex interactive dynamics, suggesting the need for better integration of explicit dialogue state management in end-to-end pipelines.
originalProblem: Most existing Spoken Dialogue Models (SDMs) operate in a half-duplex mode, leading to unnatural, sequential conversations that lack human-like fluidity.
The rapid emergence of full-duplex SDMs (which listen and speak simultaneously) has created a significant gap in suitable, standardized evaluation methodologies.

Current benchmarks primarily focus on content understanding or rely on subjective user studies, failing to systematically assess real-time interactive behaviors like fluid turn-taking and interruption handling.
results: Gemini Live achieved the lowest Takeover Rate (0.255-0.310), indicating superior pause management, and the best Jensen-Shannon Divergence (0.896) for human-like backchannel timing.
Freeze-Omni demonstrated high effectiveness in managing user interruptions, achieving a GPT-4o score of 3.615 for content coherence and adaptability.
End-to-end models (dGSLM, Moshi) exhibited high Takeover Rates (up to 0.985) and significantly struggled with maintaining coherence during interruptions, yielding GPT-4o scores as low as 0.201.
solution: Introduces "Full-Duplex-Bench," the first scenario-driven benchmark specifically designed to evaluate full-duplex SDMs on four critical interactive dimensions.

Develops an automatic evaluation framework utilizing ASR-generated, time-aligned transcriptions and objective metrics (e.g., Takeover Rate, Jensen-Shannon Divergence, latency, GPT-4o scores) for reproducible assessment.

Curates evaluation data from existing corpora (Candor, ICC) and synthetic sources to create diverse scenarios for assessing pause handling, backchanneling, smooth turn-taking, and user interruption management.
paper_summary.summary: Full-Duplex-Bench introduces a scenario-driven benchmark to evaluate full-duplex spoken dialogue models' capabilities in turn-taking, pause handling, backchanneling, and user interruption management using automatic, time-aligned metrics. The evaluation reveals that commercial systems like Gemini Live and cascaded architectures with explicit control modules generally outperform transparent end-to-end models in nuanced interactive behaviors.

title: Full-Duplex-Bench: A Benchmark to Evaluate Full-duplex Spoken Dialogue Models on Turn-taking Capabilities

abstract:
We present MultiChallenge, a pioneering benchmark evaluating large language
models (LLMs) on conducting multi-turn conversations with human users, a
crucial yet underexamined capability for their applications. MultiChallenge
identifies four categories of challenges in multi-turn conversations that are
not only common and realistic among current human-LLM interactions, but are
also challenging to all current frontier LLMs. All 4 challenges require
accurate instruction-following, context allocation, and in-context reasoning at
the same time. We also develop LLM as judge with instance-level rubrics to
facilitate an automatic evaluation method with fair agreement with experienced
human raters. Despite achieving near-perfect scores on existing multi-turn
evaluation benchmarks, all frontier models have less than 50% accuracy on
MultiChallenge, with the top-performing Claude 3.5 Sonnet (June 2024) achieving
just a 41.4% average accuracy.

keyInsights: Frontier LLMs, despite high scores on previous benchmarks, show surprisingly low accuracy (all below 50%) on MultiChallenge's realistic multi-turn scenarios, indicating significant remaining limitations in conversational abilities.

The proposed 'LLM as judge with instance-level rubrics' evaluation method achieves 93.95% alignment with human raters, validating it as a reliable and scalable approach for evaluating complex, open-ended conversational tasks.

The difficulty of multi-turn conversational challenges for LLMs is driven by intrinsic reasoning, instruction following, and context allocation rather than simply the number of turns in a conversation.
originalProblem: Existing multi-turn conversation benchmarks (e.g., MT-Bench) have become saturated, with frontier LLMs achieving near-perfect scores, making them less discriminative for identifying true progress or weaknesses.
Comprehensive evaluation of crucial multi-turn conversational capabilities in LLMs, such as sustained memory, dynamic content manipulation, and conversational consistency, remains underexamined.
There is a need for an efficient and reliable method to evaluate complex, open-ended conversational tasks where a single 'ground truth' answer is often elusive.
results: All 6 frontier LLMs evaluated scored less than 50% accuracy on MultiChallenge, with Claude 3.5 Sonnet leading at 41.4% (human-evaluated) / 42.75% (auto-evaluated), revealing significant multi-turn weaknesses.
The 'LLM as judge with instance-level rubrics' auto-evaluation achieved 93.95% average alignment with human raters, vastly outperforming a direct LLM-as-judge baseline (37.33% alignment).

The hybrid synthetic data generation with human review reduced test example creation time by approximately 52% (73.6 minutes per example vs. 154.4 minutes from scratch), with human editing significantly refining synthetic content.
solution: Introduces MultiChallenge, a novel multi-turn conversation evaluation benchmark comprising four distinct challenge categories: Instruction Retention, Inference Memory of User Information, Reliable Versioned Editing, and Self-Coherence.

Develops a hybrid data generation approach using a Multi-Agent Synthetic Data Generation (MMSE) system combined with meticulous human review and editing to create diverse, realistic, and challenging test examples.

Implements an 'LLM as judge with instance-level rubrics' automatic evaluation method where human-provided binary questions are answered by an LLM judge, enabling scalable and reliable assessment of model responses.
paper_summary.summary: Scale AI developed MultiChallenge, a new benchmark designed to evaluate Large Language Models' (LLMs) multi-turn conversational abilities, identifying critical weaknesses even in frontier models that achieve near-perfect scores on older benchmarks. The benchmark's hybrid data generation and novel LLM-as-judge evaluation method show high alignment with human assessments, providing a reliable tool for future LLM development.

title: MultiChallenge: A Realistic Multi-Turn Conversation Evaluation Benchmark  Challenging to Frontier LLMs

abstract:
Building on the success of large language models (LLMs), recent advancements
such as GPT-4o have enabled real-time speech interactions through LLM-based
voice assistants, offering a significantly improved user experience compared to
traditional text-based interactions. However, the absence of benchmarks
designed to evaluate these speech interaction capabilities has hindered
progress of LLM-based voice assistants development. Current evaluations focus
primarily on automatic speech recognition (ASR) or general knowledge evaluation
with clean speeches, neglecting the more intricate, real-world scenarios that
involve diverse speaker characteristics, environmental and content factors. To
address this, we introduce VoiceBench, the first benchmark designed to provide
a multi-faceted evaluation of LLM-based voice assistants. VoiceBench also
includes both real and synthetic spoken instructions that incorporate the above
three key real-world variations. Extensive experiments reveal the limitations
of current LLM-based voice assistant models and offer valuable insights for
future research and development in this field.

keyInsights: Current open-source end-to-end (E2E) voice assistants significantly underperform compared to traditional pipeline models (ASR + LLM) and proprietary solutions like GPT-4o-Audio, which demonstrate superior performance.
E2E models exhibit a substantial performance gap between text and speech inputs, with some also showing degraded text-based capabilities after fine-tuning for audio.
A critical safety vulnerability exists in several E2E models, which fail to reject malicious instructions delivered via speech, despite doing so for text inputs.
originalProblem: A lack of standardized, comprehensive benchmarks specifically designed for evaluating LLM-based voice assistants.

Existing evaluations narrowly focus on automatic speech recognition (ASR) performance or clean speech, neglecting real-world complexities like diverse speaker characteristics, environmental noise, and common spoken language variations.
Inadequate assessment of core LLM capabilities (general knowledge, instruction-following, safety) within a speech interaction context, especially under challenging, real-world conditions.
results: Pipeline models consistently outperformed all open-source E2E models, often by over 20 points, while GPT-4o-Audio demonstrated near pipeline-level performance as an E2E system.

E2E models like VITA experienced large performance drops (e.g., >35 points) when switching from text to speech inputs, compared to a smaller (~4.37 points) drop for pipeline models primarily due to ASR errors.

Models showed significant vulnerability to specific real-world factors, including speaking speed variations, low-resource accents, environmental noise, signal distortion, and particularly content variations like mispronunciations and speech disfluencies (e.g., repairs).
solution: VoiceBench provides a multi-faceted benchmark for LLM-based voice assistants, comprising Capability Evaluation (general knowledge, instruction-following, safety) and Robustness Evaluation components, utilizing spoken instructions.
The benchmark constructs datasets by converting filtered text instructions into speech using advanced TTS (Google TTS API) and incorporates real spoken instructions to capture diverse scenarios.

Systematically simulates real-world variations including speaker characteristics (age, accent, speaking speed), environmental conditions (noise, reverberation, far-field, distortion, packet loss), and content variations (mispronunciations, disfluencies, grammatical errors).
paper_summary.summary: VoiceBench introduces the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM-based voice assistants across general knowledge, instruction-following, and safety, under diverse real-world speaker, environmental, and content variations. It reveals that current open-source end-to-end models underperform compared to pipeline approaches and proprietary solutions, struggling with real-world complexities and exhibiting specific safety vulnerabilities in speech mode.

title: VoiceBench: Benchmarking LLM-Based Voice Assistants

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have driven significant progress in end-to-end spoken dialogue models (SDMs). In contrast to text-based LLMs, the evaluation framework for SDMs should encompass both cognitive dimensions (e.g., logical reasoning, knowledge) and speech-related aspects (e.g., paralinguistic cues, audio quality). However, there is still a lack of comprehensive evaluations for SDMs in speech-to-speech (S2S) scenarios. To address this gap, we propose URO-Bench, an extensive benchmark for SDMs. Notably, URO-Bench is the first S2S benchmark that covers evaluations about multilingualism, multi-round dialogues, and paralinguistics. Our benchmark is divided into two difficulty levels: basic track and pro track, each comprising 20 test sets, evaluating the spoken dialogue model's abilities in Understanding, Reasoning, and Oral conversation. Evaluations on our proposed benchmark reveal that current open-source SDMs perform rather well in daily QA tasks, but lag behind their backbone LLMs in terms of instruction-following ability and also suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Their performance in advanced evaluations of paralinguistic information and audio understanding remains subpar, highlighting the need for further research in this direction. We hope that URO-Bench can facilitate the development of spoken dialogue models by providing a multifaceted evaluation of existing models and helping to track progress in this area.

keyInsights: A significant performance disparity exists between open-source end-to-end SDMs and state-of-the-art proprietary models like GPT-4o-Audio-Preview, particularly in advanced reasoning and complex audio understanding.

Many open-source SDMs suffer from 'catastrophic forgetting' during S2S adaptation, leading to a substantial loss of cognitive abilities (e.g., instruction following, complex math) compared to their text-based backbone LLMs.

Current SDMs show limited audio understanding beyond spoken language (struggling with music or environmental sounds) and weak paralinguistic processing capabilities for distinguishing speakers or generating expressive speech like singing.
originalProblem: A critical gap exists in comprehensive evaluation frameworks for end-to-end speech-to-speech (S2S) dialogue models (SDMs), as traditional benchmarks focus on text-based LLMs or isolated speech components.

Existing S2S benchmarks often fail to assess crucial advanced capabilities such as multilingualism, multi-round dialogue context management, and the understanding/generation of paralinguistic features.

The rapid advancement of end-to-end S2S models, which aim to reduce latency and errors of cascaded systems, lacks a unified evaluation standard that integrates both cognitive abilities and speech-specific quality.
results: GLM-4-Voice emerged as the top open-source SDM, outperforming others by at least 10 points on the benchmark, attributed to its larger backbone LLM and extensive training.
Open-source SDMs generally performed poorly on Pro Track tasks, with very low scores in environmental sound interpretation and speaker recognition, often performing no better than random guessing.

GPT-4o-Audio-Preview consistently achieved high performance, excelling in cross-lingual conversations, understanding music/environmental sounds, and demonstrating exceptionally low WER/CER, indicating remarkable speech-text alignment even with complex outputs.
solution: The URO-Bench benchmark was developed, structured around a three-dimensional taxonomy: Understanding, Reasoning, and Oral Conversation, to cover both cognitive and speech-related dimensions.

It features 40 datasets across 20 distinct tasks, divided into Basic and Pro tracks, explicitly targeting multilingualism, multi-round dialogues, and paralinguistic feature processing (emotion, speaker recognition, environmental sounds).

A rigorous 6-step construction pipeline was implemented, involving task curation, data selection from existing benchmarks and LLM-generated conversations, state-of-the-art TTS for speech synthesis, and extensive ASR/manual filtering to ensure high-quality, diverse content.
paper_summary.summary: URO-Bench introduces the first comprehensive benchmark for end-to-end speech-to-speech dialogue models, assessing multilingualism, multi-round conversations, and paralinguistic features across both cognitive and speech dimensions. It reveals a substantial performance gap between current open-source models and proprietary systems, particularly in advanced reasoning and nuanced audio understanding.

title: URO-Bench: Towards Comprehensive Evaluation for End-to-End Spoken Dialogue Models

abstract: We introduce Gaia2, a benchmark for evaluating large language model agents in realistic, asynchronous environments. Unlike prior static or synchronous evaluations, Gaia2 introduces scenarios where environments evolve independently of agent actions, requiring agents to operate under temporal constraints, adapt to noisy and dynamic events, resolve ambiguity, and collaborate with other agents. Each scenario is paired with a write-action verifier, enabling fine-grained, action-level evaluation and making Gaia2 directly usable for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models shows that no model dominates across capabilities: GPT-5 (high) reaches the strongest overall score of 42% pass@1 but fails on time-sensitive tasks, Claude-4 Sonnet trades accuracy and speed for cost, Kimi-K2 leads among open-source models with 21% pass@1. These results highlight fundamental trade-offs between reasoning, efficiency, robustness, and expose challenges in closing the "sim2real" gap. Gaia2 is built on a consumer environment with the open-source Agents Research Environments platform and designed to be easy to extend. By releasing Gaia2 alongside the foundational ARE framework, we aim to provide the community with a flexible infrastructure for developing, benchmarking, and training the next generation of practical agent systems.

keyInsights: State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibit trade-offs, with no single model demonstrating superior performance across all capabilities like temporal reasoning, adaptability, and noise robustness.
Inference latency significantly impacts performance on time-sensitive tasks, where faster models achieved higher scores even with potentially weaker overall reasoning.
Multi-agent collaboration can benefit weaker models but may not yield cost-normalized gains for stronger agents, indicating that efficient task decomposition and delegation are crucial.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM agents do not adequately evaluate performance in dynamic, asynchronous, and complex real-world environments.
There is a lack of comprehensive, verifiable frameworks to assess capabilities like temporal reasoning, noise robustness, ambiguity resolution, and multi-agent collaboration.
Current Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods struggle due to insufficient fine-grained reward signals for complex, multi-step tasks.
results: GPT-5 (high) achieved an overall pass@1 score of 42.1%, while Kimi-K2 led open-source models with 20.1% pass@1, demonstrating current performance ceilings.

Models struggled with complex capabilities such as ambiguity resolution, adaptability, and time-sensitive tasks; for instance, GPT-5 (high) scored 0.0% on time-sensitive tasks with default inference but 34.4% when latency was eliminated.
Agent2Agent experiments indicated that weaker models benefited from increased collaboration, and heterogeneous teams (strong main agent, weaker app-agents) improved performance.
solution: An open-source Agents Research Environments (ARE) framework was developed to create asynchronous, event-driven environments for LLM agent evaluation.
The GAIA2 benchmark was constructed using ARE, comprising 1,120 scenarios that test critical capabilities in dynamic settings, including temporal reasoning and multi-agent interaction.
A novel action-level verifier was integrated to provide precise, verifiable rewards for each agent action, enabling scalable Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Meta SuperIntelligence Labs developed GAIA2, a benchmark and open-source ARE framework, to evaluate large language model agents in dynamic, asynchronous, and multi-agent environments. Empirical evaluation revealed that state-of-the-art models, including GPT-5 (high) at 42.1% pass@1 and Kimi-K2 at 20.1% pass@1 among open-source models, face significant challenges with temporal reasoning, noise robustness, and ambiguity resolution.

title: Gaia2: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Dynamic and Asynchronous Environments

abstract: Full-duplex spoken dialogue systems promise to transform human-machine interaction from a rigid, turn-based protocol into a fluid, natural conversation. However, the central challenge to realizing this vision, managing overlapping speech, remains critically under-evaluated. We introduce Full-Duplex-Bench v1.5, the first fully automated benchmark designed to systematically probe how models behave during speech overlap. The benchmark simulates four representative overlap scenarios: user interruption, user backchannel, talking to others, and background speech. Our framework, compatible with open-source and commercial API-based models, provides a comprehensive suite of metrics analyzing categorical dialogue behaviors, stop and response latency, and prosodic adaptation. Benchmarking five state-of-the-art agents reveals two divergent strategies: a responsive approach prioritizing rapid response to user input, and a floor-holding approach that preserves conversational flow by filtering overlapping events. Our open-source framework enables practitioners to accelerate the development of robust full-duplex systems by providing the tools for reproducible evaluation

keyInsights: A consistent trade-off exists between rapid responsiveness and robust floor control among state-of-the-art full-duplex models; highly responsive models often over-accommodate, while robust floor-holding models delay necessary repairs during true interruptions.

Different types of overlapping speech (e.g., interruption vs. backchannel vs. background noise) demand distinct and socially appropriate model behaviors, highlighting the need for advanced addressee detection and filtering capabilities.
Effective prosodic adaptation, such as controlled increases in speaking rate and pitch, is critical for signaling turn re-entry and maintaining natural conversational flow after an overlap event.
originalProblem: A significant lack of systematic and reproducible evaluation methodologies specifically for full-duplex spoken dialogue systems, particularly regarding their ability to manage overlapping speech.

Existing mainstream speech benchmarks primarily focus on single-turn, half-duplex interactions, which are inadequate for assessing the dynamic and concurrent nature of real-world conversational overlap.
Human evaluations, while insightful for nuanced judgments, are resource-intensive, time-consuming, and difficult to standardize or reproduce across different research studies.
results: GPT-4o demonstrated high responsiveness for user interruptions (78% 'Respond' rate, 0.23s stop latency) but showed over-accommodation in non-addressee and background speech scenarios, yielding inappropriately.

Nova Sonic and Gemini excelled in robust floor-holding, consistently resuming turns during backchannels and 'talking to others' scenarios (up to 99% 'Resume' rate) but were slower to yield during genuine interruptions.

Prosodic analysis revealed two adaptation regimes: GPT-4o and Gemini used tempo acceleration and pitch elevation (Tempo/Pitch-lift Regime), while Freeze-Omni and Nova Sonic employed reduced mean intensity with increased intensity variability (Soft-but-Dynamic Intensity Regime) to signal turn re-entry.
solution: Developed FULL-DUPLEX-BENCH V1.5, an automated, model-agnostic evaluation framework that simulates live conversational environments by streaming pre-recorded user audio to a target model while capturing its output.
Designed four distinct and controlled overlap scenarios (User Interruption, User Backchannel, User Talking to Others, Background Speech) to systematically probe different conversational capabilities.

Introduced a comprehensive suite of evaluation metrics encompassing dialogue behavior (semantic classification using GPT-4o), interaction timing (stop and response latencies), and prosodic/quality shifts (WPM, pitch, intensity, MOS) to provide granular performance insights.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from National Taiwan University, University of Washington, UC Berkeley, and Carnegie Mellon University developed FULL-DUPLEX-BENCH V1.5, an automated benchmark for evaluating how full-duplex speech models handle overlapping speech. The framework assessed five state-of-the-art models across four controlled scenarios, revealing a consistent trade-off between rapid responsiveness and robust floor control, and detailing specific prosodic adaptations during conversational repairs.

title: Full-Duplex-Bench v1.5: Evaluating Overlap Handling for Full-Duplex Speech Models

abstract: Speech large language models (SpeechLLMs) have extended human-machine interactions from the text modality to the dynamic speech domain. Spoken dialogues convey diverse information, including semantic concepts, acoustic variations, paralanguage cues, and environmental context. However, existing evaluations of speech interaction models lack instances mimicking real scenarios and predominantly focus on the performance of distinct aspects, lacking a comprehensive comparison of critical capabilities between current routines. To address this gap, we propose VocalBench to assess the speech conversational abilities, comprising around 24k carefully curated instances of both English and Mandarin across four key dimensions - semantic quality, acoustic performance, conversational abilities, and robustness, covering 14 user-oriented characters. Experiments on 27 mainstream models reveal the common challenges for current routes, and highlight the need for new insights into next-generation speech interactive systems.

keyInsights: Semantic performance in speech models is primarily driven by the underlying LLM backbone, with larger models showing improved semantic quality but limited impact on acoustic and emotional fidelity.
End-to-end SpeechLLMs consistently outperform cascaded systems in emotional empathy, whereas cascaded approaches retain a slight semantic advantage.
End-to-end models demonstrate inherent robustness against acoustic interference, while cascaded systems are susceptible to error propagation from ASR inaccuracies.
originalProblem: Existing speech interaction benchmarks neglect speech generation quality (fluency, paralinguistic fidelity) by focusing on text responses.
Current capability categorizations for speech models are ambiguous and too narrow, failing to capture holistic vocal communication.
Evaluations often adapt text-based methodologies, lacking authentic speech scenarios to properly assess conversational abilities.
results: Tiny models can achieve competitive semantic performance in reasoning and creativity but show clear limitations in factual knowledge depth.
There is a trade-off where high semantic performance can correlate with lower speech fluency and naturalness (UTMOS), indicating challenges in speech generation for complex text.

Models exhibit insufficient control over paralinguistic attributes, limited robustness to linguistic diversity (code-switching, dialects), and significant First Chunk Latency (over 500ms) affecting real-time interaction.
solution: Introduced VocalBench, a comprehensive benchmark with approximately 24,000 curated instances in English and Mandarin, covering 14 core conversational capabilities.
Developed a multi-stage pipeline for VocalBench creation, involving LLM-generated and manually curated text instances, followed by speech synthesis, quality control, and post-processing.
Evaluated 27 mainstream speech interaction models, including various SpeechLLMs, Omni-LLMs, cascade approaches, and real-time APIs, using objective and LLM-as-a-Judge metrics.
paper_summary.summary: VocalBench introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the vocal conversational abilities of speech interaction models in English and Mandarin. It assesses models across 14 distinct capabilities, including semantic, acoustic, conversational, and robustness aspects, using approximately 24,000 curated instances to diagnose strengths and weaknesses in current systems like SpeechLLMs, Omni-LLMs, and cascade approaches.

title: VocalBench: Benchmarking the Vocal Conversational Abilities for Speech Interaction Models

abstract: Evaluations of audio-language models (ALMs) -- multimodal models that take interleaved audio and text as input and output text -- are hindered by the lack of standardized benchmarks; most benchmarks measure only one or two capabilities and omit evaluative aspects such as fairness or safety. Furthermore, comparison across models is difficult as separate evaluations test a limited number of models and use different prompting methods and inference parameters. To address these shortfalls, we introduce AHELM, a benchmark that aggregates various datasets -- including 2 new synthetic audio-text datasets called PARADE, which evaluates the ALMs on avoiding stereotypes, and CoRe-Bench, which measures reasoning over conversational audio through inferential multi-turn question answering -- to holistically measure the performance of ALMs across 10 aspects we have identified as important to the development and usage of ALMs: audio perception, knowledge, reasoning, emotion detection, bias, fairness, multilinguality, robustness, toxicity, and safety. We also standardize the prompts, inference parameters, and evaluation metrics to ensure equitable comparisons across models. We test 14 open-weight and closed-API ALMs from 3 developers and 3 additional simple baseline systems each consisting of an automatic speech recognizer and a language model. Our results show that while Gemini 2.5 Pro ranks top in 5 out of 10 aspects, it exhibits group unfairness ($p=0.01$) on ASR tasks whereas most of the other models do not. We also find that the baseline systems perform reasonably well on AHELM, with one ranking 6th overall despite having only speech-to-text capabilities. For transparency, all raw prompts, model generations, and outputs are available on our website at this https URL. AHELM is intended to be a living benchmark and new datasets and models will be added over time.
title: AHELM: A Holistic Evaluation of Audio-Language Models

abstract:
Developing large audio language models (LMs) capable of understanding diverse
spoken interactions is essential for accommodating the multimodal nature of
human communication and can increase the accessibility of language technologies
across different user populations. Recent work on audio LMs has primarily
evaluated their performance on short audio segments, typically under 30
seconds, with limited exploration of long-form conversational speech segments
that more closely reflect natural user interactions with these models. We
introduce Brutally Long Audio Bench (BLAB), a challenging long-form audio
benchmark that evaluates audio LMs on localization, duration estimation,
emotion, and counting tasks using audio segments averaging 51 minutes in
length. BLAB consists of 833+ hours of diverse, full-length audio clips, each
paired with human-annotated, text-based natural language questions and answers.
Our audio data were collected from permissively licensed sources and underwent
a human-assisted filtering process to ensure task compliance. We evaluate six
open-source and proprietary audio LMs on BLAB and find that all of them,
including advanced models such as Gemini 2.0 Pro and GPT-4o, struggle with the
tasks in BLAB. Our comprehensive analysis reveals key insights into the
trade-offs between task difficulty and audio duration. In general, we find that
audio LMs struggle with long-form speech, with performance declining as
duration increases. They perform poorly on localization, temporal reasoning,
counting, and struggle to understand non-phonemic information, relying more on
prompts than audio content. BLAB serves as a challenging evaluation framework
to develop audio LMs with robust long-form audio understanding capabilities.
title: BLAB: Brutally Long Audio Bench

abstract: While full-duplex speech agents enable natural, low-latency interaction by speaking and listening simultaneously, their consistency and task performance in multi-turn settings remain underexplored. We introduce Full-Duplex-Bench-v2 (FDB-v2), a streaming framework that integrates with an automated examiner that enforces staged goals under two pacing setups (Fast vs. Slow). FDB-v2 covers four task families: daily, correction, entity tracking, and safety. We report turn-taking fluency, multi-turn instruction following, and task-specific competence. The framework is extensible, supporting both commercial APIs and open source models. When we test full-duplex systems with FDB-v2, they often get confused when people talk at the same time, struggle to handle corrections smoothly, and sometimes lose track of who or what is being talked about. Through an open-sourced, standardized streaming protocol and a task set, FDB-v2 makes it easy to extend to new task families, allowing the community to tailor and accelerate evaluation of multi-turn full-duplex systems.

keyInsights: Full-duplex systems demonstrate a pervasive degradation in turn-taking fluency and instruction following scores as multi-turn dialogues advance.
The examiner's pacing critically influences system performance, with slow pacing often enabling better recovery in instruction following and improved scores in memory-intensive tasks.
Handling dynamic corrections and accurately tracking entities across turns remain the most challenging capabilities for current full-duplex dialogue systems.
originalProblem: Traditional spoken dialogue systems operated in a half-duplex manner, causing unnatural pauses and hindering fluid conversations.
A critical gap existed in evaluating full-duplex systems over multiple turns, particularly regarding context consistency, correction handling, and task completion across extended interactions.
Existing human evaluations are costly and not reproducible, while automated methods often miss semantic appropriateness or do not account for overlapping speech.
results: GPT-Realtime, a commercial model, consistently achieved superior performance across all tasks and pacing scenarios, maintaining scores above 4.0 in robustness tests.
Open-source models like Moshi and Freeze-Omni scored below 3.0 in Correction and Entity Tracking under fast pacing, indicating significant struggles with dynamic context updates.

Slower examiner pacing resulted in performance improvements for open-source models, specifically +0.6 for Moshi in Correction and +1.1 in Entity Tracking, suggesting benefits from increased processing time.
solution: Develops an Automated Examiner, a spoken language model (gpt-realtime), to dynamically drive multi-turn dialogues, adapting instructions and pacing (fast/slow).
Implements a streaming interaction orchestrator using a WebRTC-based framework for low-latency, bidirectional audio communication between the examiner and the system under test.

Features four task families (Daily, Correction, Entity Tracking, Safety) to assess diverse multi-turn capabilities, and employs an LLM-as-a-judge (gemini-2.5-flash) for comprehensive metric collection.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from National Taiwan University, Carnegie Mellon University, and Massachusetts Institute of Technology introduce Full-Duplex-Bench-v2, an automated, multi-turn evaluation framework for full-duplex dialogue systems with an AI examiner. Experiments using the framework reveal that current full-duplex systems experience performance degradation over multi-turn dialogues, particularly in instruction following, correction handling, and entity tracking, with commercial models showing greater robustness than open-source counterparts.

title: Full-Duplex-Bench-v2: A Multi-Turn Evaluation Framework for Duplex Dialogue Systems with an Automated Examiner

abstract: The rapid advancement of speech-to-speech (S2S) large language models (LLMs) has significantly improved real-time spoken interaction. However, current evaluation frameworks remain inadequate for assessing performance in complex, multi-turn dialogues. To address this, we introduce MTalk-Bench, a multi-turn S2S benchmark covering three core dimensions: Semantic Information, Paralinguistic Information, and Ambient Sound. Each dimension includes nine realistic scenarios, along with targeted tasks to assess specific capabilities such as reasoning. Our dual-method evaluation framework combines Arena-style evaluation (pairwise comparison) and Rubrics-based evaluation (absolute scoring) for relative and absolute assessment. The benchmark includes both model and human outputs, evaluated by human evaluators and LLMs. Experimental results reveal two sets of findings. Overall performance of S2S LLMs: (1) models excel at semantic information processing yet underperform on paralinguistic information and ambient sounds perception; (2) models typically regain coherence by increasing response length, sacrificing efficiency in multi-turn dialogues; (3) modality-aware, task-specific designs outperform brute scaling. Evaluation framework and reliability: (1) Arena and Rubrics yield consistent, complementary rankings, but reliable distinctions emerge only when performance gaps are large; (2) LLM-as-a-judge aligns with humans when gaps are clear or criteria explicit, but exhibits position and length biases and is reliable on nonverbal evaluation only with text annotations. These results highlight current limitations in S2S evaluation and the need for more robust, speech-aware assessment frameworks.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: S2S LLMs generally excel at semantic processing but show significantly poorer performance when handling paralinguistic information and ambient sounds, highlighting a gap in multimodal understanding.
Models often compensate for early-turn context-accumulation bottlenecks in multi-turn dialogues by generating longer, more verbose responses, sacrificing conversational efficiency for coherence.
Task-specific architectural designs that exploit modality strengths and relieve early context pressure are more effective for S2S performance than merely scaling up parameter counts.
originalProblem: The rapid advancement of Speech-to-Speech (S2S) LLMs has outpaced the development of adequate, holistic evaluation frameworks.
Existing benchmarks are fragmented, often focusing on single-turn tasks or isolated sub-tasks, failing to assess end-to-end S2S performance in complex, multi-turn dialogues.
A unified framework was lacking that could evaluate S2S models across semantic, paralinguistic, and ambient acoustic dimensions using fully audio-grounded inputs.
results: Current S2S LLMs generally outperform human baselines in semantic information processing but experience a significant decline when processing paralinguistic information and a further drop for ambient sounds.

LLM-as-a-Judge evaluators show measurable biases, including a strong preference for longer responses (up to +17.3% length bias) and position bias, although they align with human judgments for clear performance differences.

The reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge for non-verbal cues is highly dependent on input modality, demonstrating near-zero agreement with humans for raw audio but high agreement (e.g., Spearman ρ > 0.85) when cues are provided as explicit text annotations.
solution: Designed MTalk-Bench, the first benchmark for holistic, speech-native evaluation of multi-turn S2S dialogue across semantic, paralinguistic, and ambient sound dimensions, derived from user-centric scenarios and a hierarchical capability taxonomy.

Proposed a novel dual-method evaluation framework combining pairwise arena-style (relative, Elo rating) and pointwise rubrics-based (absolute, fine-grained) assessment protocols for comprehensive analysis.

Constructed a high-quality, multi-stage dataset featuring over 1,500 authentic multi-turn dialogues, augmented with paralinguistic and ambient sound annotations, and recorded by native English speakers.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, introduced MTalk-Bench, the first benchmark designed for holistic evaluation of Speech-to-Speech LLMs in multi-turn dialogues. It revealed that current S2S models excel semantically but struggle with paralinguistic and ambient audio cues, and showed LLM-as-a-judge evaluators exhibit biases and limited reliability for subtle audio distinctions.

title: MTalk-Bench: Evaluating Speech-to-Speech Models in Multi-Turn Dialogues via Arena-style and Rubrics Protocols

abstract: Spoken Dialogue Models (SDMs) have recently attracted significant attention for their ability to generate voice responses directly to users' spoken queries. Despite their increasing popularity, there exists a gap in research focused on comprehensively understanding their practical effectiveness in comprehending and emulating human conversations. This is especially true compared to text-based Large Language Models (LLMs), which benefit from extensive benchmarking. Human voice interactions are inherently more complex than text due to characteristics unique to spoken dialogue. Ambiguity poses one challenge, stemming from semantic factors like polysemy, as well as phonological aspects such as heterograph, heteronyms, and stress patterns. Additionally, context-dependency, like omission, coreference, and multi-turn interaction, adds further complexity to human conversational dynamics. To illuminate the current state of SDM development and to address these challenges, we present a benchmark dataset in this paper, which comprises 1,079 instances in English and Chinese. Accompanied by an LLM-based evaluation method that closely aligns with human judgment, this dataset facilitates a comprehensive exploration of the performance of SDMs in tackling these practical challenges.
title: C3: A Bilingual Benchmark for Spoken Dialogue Models Exploring Challenges in Complex Conversations

abstract:
Evaluating the performance of LLMs in multi-turn human-agent interactions
presents significant challenges, particularly due to the complexity and
variability of user behavior. In this paper, we introduce HammerBench, a novel
benchmark framework for assessing LLMs' function-calling capabilities in
real-world, multi-turn dialogues. HammerBench simulates diverse mobile
assistant use cases, incorporating imperfect instructions, dynamic
question-answer trajectories, intent and argument shifts, and the indirect use
of external information through pronouns. To construct this benchmark, we
curate a comprehensive dataset derived from popular mobile app functionalities
and anonymized user logs, complemented by a cost-effective data generation
pipeline leveraging open-source models. HammerBench is further augmented with
fine-grained interaction snapshots and metrics, enabling detailed evaluation of
function-calling performance across individual conversational turns. We
demonstrate the effectiveness of HammerBench by evaluating several leading LLMs
and uncovering key performance trends. Our experiments reveal that different
types of parameter name errors are a significant source of failure across
different interaction scenarios, highlighting critical areas for further
improvement in LLM robustness for mobile assistant applications.

keyInsights: Real-world mobile assistant function calling demands robust handling of dynamic, imperfect, and multi-turn interactions, which standard benchmarks often oversimplify.
Decoupling function call recognition at each turn from question generation significantly improves diagnostic capabilities for identifying specific model weaknesses.

Fine-grained evaluation metrics are crucial for understanding why LLMs fail, providing actionable insights into issues like parameter hallucination under incomplete context or difficulty with anaphora resolution.
Simulating authentic user behavior and conversational complexities is essential for developing LLM agents that are truly reliable and user-friendly in practical applications.
originalProblem: Existing LLM benchmarks for function calling lacked authenticity, often relying on synthetic data or virtual environments that did not reflect real mobile user behavior.
Many evaluations failed to cover the diverse and complex conversational phenomena common in multi-turn interactions, such as intent shifts, argument shifts, and indirect references.
Current methods offered only coarse-grained assessment, making it difficult to pinpoint specific points of failure or diagnose underlying issues within a conversation.
LLMs struggled to maintain context, understand evolving user intent, and make accurate function calls throughout complex, multi-turn exchanges with imperfect instructions.
results: Proprietary models like GPT-4o and Claude3.5-sonnet generally demonstrated superior performance in complex multi-turn function-calling tasks compared to open-source LLMs.

LLMs exhibited substantial challenges with argument shifts (e.g., slot overriding, API repurposing) and handling external individual information, leading to inconsistencies and higher Parameter Missing Rates (PMR).
The Parameter Hallucination Rate (PHR) was significantly higher in initial conversational turns with incomplete context than in final turns, indicating that uncertainty drives hallucination.
Parameter name errors emerged as a notable source of failure across different interaction scenarios, highlighting a critical area for improving LLM robustness.
solution: HammerBench constructed its test data using a four-stage pipeline, drawing from popular mobile app functionalities and anonymized user logs to ensure authenticity.
The benchmark introduced "Function Calling Snapshots" to decouple information recognition from question generation, enabling detailed turn-by-turn analysis of function calls.
It encompassed diverse multi-turn scenarios including imperfect instructions, varied question-answer trajectories, explicit intent/argument shifts, and references to external individual information.
A suite of fine-grained evaluation metrics was defined, allowing for precise measurement of accuracy, parameter hallucination, missing parameters, and conversational progress.
paper_summary.summary: OPPO Research Institute and Shanghai Jiao Tong University developed HammerBench, a fine-grained benchmark for evaluating large language models' function-calling capabilities in real mobile assistant scenarios. It utilizes authentic multi-turn dialogue data to assess handling of evolving user intents and external references, revealing common failure modes such as parameter hallucination in incomplete contexts and difficulties with argument shifts.

title: HammerBench: Fine-Grained Function-Calling Evaluation in Real Mobile  Device Scenarios

abstract: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs), such as GPT-4o, have recently unlocked audio dialogue capabilities, enabling direct spoken exchanges with humans. The potential of LALMs broadens their applicability across a wide range of practical scenarios supported by audio dialogues. However, given these advancements, a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate the performance of LALMs in the open-ended audio dialogue understanding remains absent currently. To address this gap, we propose an Audio Dialogue Understanding Benchmark (ADU-Bench), which consists of 4 benchmark datasets. They assess the open-ended audio dialogue ability for LALMs in 3 general scenarios, 12 skills, 9 multilingual languages, and 4 categories of ambiguity handling. Notably, we firstly propose the evaluation of ambiguity handling in audio dialogues that expresses different intentions beyond the same literal meaning of sentences, e.g., "Really!?" with different intonations. In summary, ADU-Bench includes over 20,000 open-ended audio dialogues for the assessment of LALMs. Through extensive experiments on 16 LALMs, our analysis reveals that existing LALMs struggle with mathematical symbols and formulas, understanding human behavior such as roleplay, comprehending multiple languages, and handling audio dialogue ambiguities from different phonetic elements, such as intonations, pause positions, and homophones. The benchmark is available at this https URL.
title: Benchmarking Open-ended Audio Dialogue Understanding for Large Audio-Language Models

abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) in clinical dialogue demands robustness to full-duplex interaction, speaker overlap, and low-latency constraints, yet open benchmarks remain scarce. We present MMedFD, the first real-world Chinese healthcare ASR corpus designed for multi-turn, full-duplex settings. Captured from a deployed AI assistant, the dataset comprises 5,805 annotated sessions with synchronized user and mixed-channel views, RTTM/CTM timing, and role labels. We introduce a model-agnostic pipeline for streaming segmentation, speaker attribution, and dialogue memory, and fine-tune Whisper-small on role-concatenated audio for long-context recognition. ASR evaluation includes WER, CER, and HC-WER, which measures concept-level accuracy across healthcare settings. LLM-generated responses are assessed using rubric-based and pairwise protocols. MMedFD establishes a reproducible framework for benchmarking streaming ASR and end-to-end duplex agents in healthcare deployment. The dataset and related resources are publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: Real-world user speech in full-duplex healthcare dialogues presents substantial challenges for ASR, leading to high error rates even for fine-tuned advanced models.
Agent speech is significantly more accurate, demonstrating the contrast between synthetic, controlled audio and spontaneous human communication complexities.

Current leading large language models (LLMs) face considerable difficulties in reliably generating medically correct and contextually appropriate responses from ASR transcripts in multi-turn healthcare settings.
originalProblem: A scarcity of open, real-world, Chinese healthcare Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) benchmarks that adequately represent multi-turn, full-duplex interactions with overlapping speech.
Existing healthcare ASR corpora often rely on simulated or read speech, lacking the complexity of spontaneous clinical dialogues.
Traditional ASR evaluation metrics like Word Error Rate (WER) and Character Error Rate (CER) do not sufficiently capture the accuracy of critical medical concepts.
results: The MMEDFD corpus establishes a unique benchmark of 136.9 hours of real-world Chinese healthcare dialogues for full-duplex, multi-turn ASR evaluation.
Fine-tuned ASR models achieved a Word Error Rate (WER) of 53.11% and Healthcare-WER (HC-WER) of 15.37% for user speech, contrasting with 1.84% WER for agent speech.

Leading large language models (LLMs) exhibited high "Any-Failure%" (92.2% to 99.8%) in PairEval and average G-Eval correctness/safety scores (around 35-38%), highlighting a persistent challenge in generating reliable responses from real-world ASR transcripts.
solution: Introduction of MMEDFD, a publicly available corpus of 136.9 hours from 1,805 real-world Chinese healthcare dialogues, featuring multi-turn, full-duplex interactions with detailed annotations.
Proposal of a model-agnostic full-duplex pipeline that manages streaming segmentation, speaker diarization, context packaging, and cross-turn memory for real-time barge-in and overlapping speech.

Development of a comprehensive evaluation protocol incorporating standard ASR metrics, a novel Healthcare-Word Error Rate (HC-WER) for medical concepts, and LLM-based assessments of dialogue coherence and response quality.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The Hong Kong Polytechnic University and AntGroup introduce MMEDFD, the first real-world, multi-turn, full-duplex Chinese healthcare Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) benchmark, collected from a deployed AI assistant. This new corpus and evaluation pipeline enable more realistic assessment of ASR and large language models (LLMs) in complex clinical dialogues, demonstrating significant challenges in transcribing user speech and ensuring medical correctness in LLM responses.

title: MMedFD: A Real-world Healthcare Benchmark for Multi-turn Full-Duplex Automatic Speech Recognition

abstract:
Human feedback is crucial in the interactions between humans and Large
Language Models (LLMs). However, existing research primarily focuses on
benchmarking LLMs in single-turn dialogues. Even in benchmarks designed for
multi-turn dialogues, the user inputs are often independent, neglecting the
nuanced and complex nature of human feedback within real-world usage scenarios.
To fill this research gap, we introduce FB-Bench, a fine-grained, multi-task
benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs' responsiveness to human feedback under
real-world usage scenarios in Chinese. Drawing from the two main interaction
scenarios, FB-Bench comprises 591 meticulously curated samples, encompassing
eight task types, five deficiency types of response, and nine feedback types.
We extensively evaluate a broad array of popular LLMs, revealing significant
variations in their performance across different interaction scenarios. Further
analysis indicates that task, human feedback, and deficiencies of previous
responses can also significantly impact LLMs' responsiveness. Our findings
underscore both the strengths and limitations of current models, providing
valuable insights and directions for future research. Code and datasets are
available at this https URL
title: FB-Bench: A Fine-Grained Multi-Task Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs'  Responsiveness to Human Feedback

abstract: With the rapid integration of advanced reasoning capabilities into spoken dialogue models, the field urgently demands benchmarks that transcend simple interactions to address real-world complexity. However, current evaluations predominantly adhere to text-generation standards, overlooking the unique audio-centric characteristics of paralinguistics and colloquialisms, alongside the cognitive depth required by modern agents. To bridge this gap, we introduce WavBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate realistic conversational abilities where prior works fall short. Uniquely, WavBench establishes a tripartite framework: 1) Pro subset, designed to rigorously challenge reasoning-enhanced models with significantly increased difficulty; 2) Basic subset, defining a novel standard for spoken colloquialism that prioritizes "listenability" through natural vocabulary, linguistic fluency, and interactive rapport, rather than rigid written accuracy; and 3) Acoustic subset, covering explicit understanding, generation, and implicit dialogue to rigorously evaluate comprehensive paralinguistic capabilities within authentic real-world scenarios. Through evaluating five state-of-the-art models, WavBench offers critical insights into the intersection of complex problem-solving, colloquial delivery, and paralinguistic fidelity, guiding the evolution of robust spoken dialogue models. The benchmark dataset and evaluation toolkit are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A significant 'Cognitive-Acoustic Alignment' gap exists, where leading models struggle to effectively simplify intricate logic for natural auditory comprehension.
All evaluated models demonstrate a bottleneck in maintaining consistent paralinguistic style (EQ) across multi-turn implicit dialogues, despite improving semantic coherence (IQ).
Models exhibit limited capabilities in understanding and generating fine-grained prosodic features like pitch, volume, and accent, as well as complex background acoustic environments.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for end-to-end spoken dialogue models primarily follow text-generation standards, neglecting crucial audio-centric conversational characteristics.
Current evaluations inadequately test advanced reasoning capabilities in a spoken context, often failing to assess the clarity and listenability of complex logic.

There is a lack of comprehensive assessment for colloquial expression and paralinguistic fidelity, including the generation and understanding of nuanced vocal features and natural conversational style in dynamic interactions.
results: GPT-4o Audio achieved the highest scores in Colloquial Expression (Pro: 58.23, Basic: 68.80), demonstrating superior reasoning and colloquial delivery in spoken contexts.

Models showed high accuracy in Language ID and Emotion Recognition but limited capability in understanding and generating fine-grained prosodic features (e.g., Pitch, Accent, Volume) and background audio.
Multi-turn implicit dialogues revealed a challenge for models to maintain paralinguistic consistency, with text scores improving but audio scores sharply declining from single-turn interactions.
solution: Developed WavBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 17,577 high-quality audio items, structured into Colloquial Expression and Acoustic Interaction sets.
The Colloquial Expression set adapts diverse cognitive tasks (e.g., Math, Code) into natural spoken inquiries and conversational replies, verified by human annotators.

The Acoustic Interaction set includes dialogues with both explicit and implicit paralinguistic instructions, using advanced TTS with attribute-specific conditioning for 10 dimensions like age, emotion, and accent.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce WavBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate end-to-end spoken dialogue models across reasoning, colloquialism, and paralinguistics, featuring 17,577 high-quality audio items. The benchmark reveals that even leading models struggle with effectively simplifying complex reasoning for auditory comprehension and maintaining consistent paralinguistic style in multi-turn dialogues.

title: WavBench: Benchmarking Reasoning, Colloquialism, and Paralinguistics for End-to-End Spoken Dialogue Models

abstract: Full-duplex voice agents--systems that listen and speak simultaneously--are rapidly moving from research to production. However, existing evaluations address conversational dynamics and task completion in isolation. We introduce $\tau$-voice, a benchmark for evaluating voice agents on grounded tasks with real-world complexity: agents must navigate complex multi-turn conversations, adhere to domain policies, and interact with the environment. The framework extends $\tau^2$-bench into a novel voice agent benchmark combining verifiable completion of complex grounded tasks, full-duplex interaction, and realistic audio--enabling direct comparison between voice and text performance. A controllable and realistic voice user simulator provides diverse accents, realistic audio environments, and rich turn-taking dynamics; by decoupling simulation from wall-clock time, the user simulator can use the most capable LLM without real-time constraints. We evaluate task completion (pass@1) and voice interaction quality across 278 tasks: while GPT-5 (reasoning) achieves 85%, voice agents reach only 31--51% under clean conditions and 26--38% under realistic conditions with noise and diverse accents--retaining only 30--45% of text capability; qualitative analysis confirms 79--90% of failures stem from agent behavior, suggesting that observed failures primarily reflect agent behavior under our evaluation setup. $\tau$-voice provides a reproducible testbed for measuring progress toward voice agents that are natural, conversational, and reliable.

keyInsights: A substantial performance gap exists between voice agents and text-based Large Language Models, with voice agents achieving only 30-45% of the task completion capability of text LLMs under realistic conditions.
Diverse accents significantly degrade voice agent performance, causing an average 10 percentage point drop in task success, with certain providers showing severe vulnerability.

Current voice agents exhibit distinct behavioral trade-offs in conversational dynamics, balancing responsiveness and interruption rates against selectivity, without a single agent excelling comprehensively.
originalProblem: Existing voice agent benchmarks either focus on task completion in text-only settings or on conversational dynamics in synthetic environments, lacking a holistic evaluation of real-world performance.

The inherent complexities of full-duplex voice interaction, such as real-time turn-taking, disfluencies, and challenging acoustic conditions, are not adequately captured by fragmented evaluation methodologies.
There was no unified framework to directly compare voice agent performance against text-based systems on consequential tasks, hindering a comprehensive understanding of system-level limitations.
results: Voice agents achieved 31–51% task completion under clean conditions, dropping to 26–38% under realistic conditions, representing a 34–54 percentage point deficit relative to text-based GPT-5.
Ablation studies revealed diverse accents as the most impactful factor, causing an average 10pp drop in pass@1, with xAI experiencing an 18pp drop and Google only a 1pp drop.

OpenAI demonstrated the lowest response latency (0.90s) and highest responsiveness (100%) but low selectivity (6%), while xAI showed the best selectivity (57%) but the highest agent interruption rate (84%).
solution: τ-Voice extends the τ^2-bench framework by introducing a benchmark for full-duplex voice agents on grounded tasks, integrating real-world complexity.
The methodology includes a full-duplex orchestrator that manages continuous audio exchange and decouples simulation time for reproducibility, and a realistic voice user simulator.
The voice user simulator generates natural spoken language with disfluencies, simulates diverse audio environments (noise, accents, telephony effects), and employs LLM-driven turn-taking policies.
paper_summary.summary: τ-Voice introduces a benchmark to evaluate full-duplex voice agents on grounded, real-world tasks by integrating complex conversational dynamics with realistic audio conditions. Evaluations using this framework show that current voice agents experience a significant performance drop compared to text-based LLMs, particularly when facing diverse accents and noisy environments.

title: $τ$-Voice: Benchmarking Full-Duplex Voice Agents on Real-World Domains

abstract: End-to-end (E2E) spoken dialogue systems are increasingly replacing cascaded pipelines for voice-based human-AI interaction, processing raw audio directly without intermediate transcription. Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate these models on synthetic speech and single-turn tasks, leaving realistic multi-turn conversational ability underexplored. We introduce Audio MultiChallenge, an open-source benchmark to evaluate E2E spoken dialogue systems under natural multi-turn interaction patterns. Building on the text-based MultiChallenge framework, which evaluates Inference Memory, Instruction Retention, and Self Coherence, we introduce a new axis Voice Editing that tests robustness to mid-utterance speech repairs and backtracking. We further augment each axis to the audio modality, such as introducing Audio-Cue challenges for Inference Memory that require recalling ambient sounds and paralinguistic signals beyond semantic content. We curate 452 conversations from 47 speakers with 1,712 instance-specific rubrics through a hybrid audio-native agentic and human-in-the-loop pipeline that exposes model failures at scale while preserving natural disfluencies found in unscripted human speech. Our evaluation of proprietary and open-source models reveals that even frontier models struggle on our benchmark, with Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking), our highest-performing model achieving a 54.65% pass rate. Error analysis shows that models fail most often on our new axes and that Self Coherence degrades with longer audio context. These failures reflect difficulty of tracking edits, audio cues, and long-range context in natural spoken dialogue. Audio MultiChallenge provides a reproducible testbed to quantify them and drive improvements in audio-native multi-turn interaction capability.

keyInsights: Even frontier E2E spoken dialogue models exhibit substantial shortcomings in handling realistic multi-turn conversations, especially with audio-native challenges.

Voice editing (mid-utterance speech repairs) and recalling non-semantic audio cues from conversation history proved to be particularly difficult for models, indicating a fundamental gap in audio intelligence.

A 'modality gap' was observed, with models performing worse when generating audio outputs compared to text outputs, and cascaded systems struggling significantly with audio-native cues compared to E2E systems.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for end-to-end (E2E) spoken dialogue systems primarily rely on synthetic speech, clean audio, or single-turn tasks, failing to reflect real-world conversational complexities.
Current evaluation methods do not adequately assess models on natural multi-turn human speech, which includes disfluencies, interruptions, and the need for sustained context tracking.
There is a lack of rigorous evaluation specifically designed for audio-native challenges, such as handling mid-utterance speech repairs (voice editing) and recalling non-semantic audio cues.
results: The top-performing model achieved only a 54.65% Average Pass Rate, while many open-source models scored below 30%, underscoring the significant room for improvement in current systems.
The 'Voice Editing' (17.99% APR) and 'Inference Memory' (21.55% APR) axes were identified as the most challenging, indicating specific areas where models struggle the most.
Performance on Audio-Cue Inference Memory tasks showed a 36.5% relative decline compared to semantic memory tasks, and Self Coherence degraded as the cumulative user audio duration increased.
solution: Developed Audio MultiChallenge, an open-source benchmark dataset designed for the multi-turn evaluation of E2E spoken dialogue systems under natural human interaction patterns.
Introduced novel evaluation axes like 'Voice Editing' and 'Audio-Cue Inference Memory,' alongside adapted 'Inference Memory,' 'Instruction Retention,' and 'Self Coherence' axes.
Utilized a hybrid data generation pipeline combining multi-agent LLM-based failure mode discovery with human-in-the-loop, unscripted conversation creation to ensure naturalism and scalability.
paper_summary.summary: Audio MultiChallenge introduces an open-source benchmark to evaluate end-to-end spoken dialogue systems on multi-turn, natural human interactions. It demonstrates that frontier models achieve a maximum Average Pass Rate of 54.65% on these challenging tasks, particularly struggling with voice editing and audio-cue inference memory, thereby revealing current limitations in voice AI capabilities.

title: Audio MultiChallenge: A Multi-Turn Evaluation of Spoken Dialogue Systems on Natural Human Interaction

abstract: Current speech evaluation suffers from two critical limitations: the need and difficulty of designing specialized systems targeting individual audio characteristics, and poor correlation between automatic evaluation methods and human preferences. This work presents a systematic study of Large Audio Model (LAM) as a Judge, AudioJudge, investigating whether it can provide a unified evaluation framework that addresses both challenges. We systematically explore AudioJudge across audio characteristic detection tasks, including pronunciation, speaking rate, speaker identification and speech quality, and system-level human preference simulation for automated benchmarking. We investigate different prompt engineering strategies, finding that audio concatenation combined with in-context learning significantly improves performance across both audio characteristic detection and human preference simulation tasks. We further introduce a multi-aspect ensemble AudioJudge to enable general-purpose multi-aspect audio evaluation. This method decomposes speech assessment into specialized judges for lexical content, speech quality, and paralinguistic features, achieving up to 0.91 Spearman correlation with human preferences on our system ranking benchmark. Robustness analysis reveals that while LAMs maintain strong performance under acoustic noise, they exhibit significant verbosity and positional biases that require careful mitigation.

keyInsights: Audio concatenation, particularly concatenating both in-context examples and test audios, is critical for enabling Large Audio Models (LAMs) to perform effectively in speech evaluation tasks requiring in-context learning.

LAMs can achieve strong correlations with human preferences in system-level speech evaluation (up to Spearman's ρ=0.93), especially when utilizing multi-aspect ensembles for complex tasks, demonstrating their potential for automated benchmarking.

LAMs exhibit robustness to acoustic noise in their predictions but show biases towards verbosity and positional order (especially recency bias for non-lexical tasks), which can influence judgments in ambiguous cases.
originalProblem: Traditional speech evaluation requires training and deploying separate, specialized models for each audio characteristic (e.g., quality, pronunciation, rate), leading to high development costs and fragmented evaluation pipelines.

Existing automatic speech evaluation methods often show poor correlation with nuanced human judgments, while manual human evaluation is prohibitively expensive and time-consuming for large-scale benchmarking.
The absence of a unified, efficient, and human-aligned framework hinders rapid iteration and comparison of speech generation systems.
results: AudioJudge, with optimal prompt engineering (Examples & Test Concatenation 4-shot), improved pronunciation detection from 46.0% to 66.5% and speaker identification from 61.5% to 70.0% accuracy.

System-level evaluation demonstrated high human preference correlation, with GPT-4o-Audio achieving a Spearman's ρ of 0.931 on ChatbotArena-Spoken and Gemini-2.5-Flash reaching 0.912 on SpeakBench with a multi-aspect ensemble.

LAMs maintained high prediction stability (85-93% unchanged) even at 1 dB Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), but exhibited a statistically significant preference for longer responses and notable positional biases, particularly for speaking rate tasks.
solution: AudioJudge leverages Large Audio Models (LAMs) as evaluators within a pairwise comparison framework, prompting them to assess two audio responses against an instruction.

The approach introduces novel prompt engineering strategies, including in-context audio concatenation (e.g., concatenating examples and test audios into a single stream) to enhance LAM processing of multi-segment audio.

For complex evaluations, a multi-aspect judge ensemble is proposed, where specialized judges (lexical, paralinguistic, speech quality) independently assess different aspects, with final decisions determined by majority voting.
paper_summary.summary: A study explores using Large Audio Models (LAMs) as "AudioJudge" to provide a unified framework for speech evaluation, aiming to replace specialized models and better correlate with human preferences. The research identifies audio concatenation and multi-aspect ensembles as effective prompt engineering strategies, achieving up to 0.93 Spearman correlation with human judgments in system-level evaluations.

title: AudioJudge: Understanding What Works in Large Audio Model Based Speech Evaluation

abstract:
The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has brought significant
attention to speech models, particularly recent progress in speech2speech
protocols supporting speech input and output. However, the existing benchmarks
adopt automatic text-based evaluators for evaluating the instruction following
ability of these models lack consideration for paralinguistic information in
both speech understanding and generation. To address these issues, we introduce
S2S-Arena, a novel arena-style S2S benchmark that evaluates
instruction-following capabilities with paralinguistic information in both
speech-in and speech-out across real-world tasks. We design 154 samples that
fused TTS and live recordings in four domains with 21 tasks and manually
evaluate existing popular speech models in an arena-style manner. The
experimental results show that: (1) in addition to the superior performance of
GPT-4o, the speech model of cascaded ASR, LLM, and TTS outperforms the jointly
trained model after text-speech alignment in speech2speech protocols; (2)
considering paralinguistic information, the knowledgeability of the speech
model mainly depends on the LLM backbone, and the multilingual support of that
is limited by the speech module; (3) excellent speech models can already
understand the paralinguistic information in speech input, but generating
appropriate audio with paralinguistic information is still a challenge.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

title: S2S-Arena, Evaluating Speech2Speech Protocols on Instruction Following  with Paralinguistic Information

abstract: Conversational Spoken Language Models (SLMs) are emerging as a promising paradigm for real-time speech interaction. However, their capacity of temporal dynamics, including the ability to manage timing, tempo and simultaneous speaking, remains a critical and unevaluated challenge for conversational fluency. To address this gap, we introduce the Game-Time Benchmark, a framework to systematically assess these temporal capabilities. Inspired by how humans learn a language through language activities, Game-Time consists of basic instruction-following tasks and advanced tasks with temporal constraints, such as tempo adherence and synchronized responses. Our evaluation of diverse SLM architectures reveals a clear performance disparity: while state-of-the-art models handle basic tasks well, many contemporary systems still struggle with fundamental instruction-following. More critically, nearly all models degrade substantially under temporal constraints, exposing persistent weaknesses in time awareness and full-duplex interaction. The Game-Time Benchmark provides a foundation for guiding future research toward more temporally-aware conversational AI. Demos and datasets are available on our project website this https URL.
title: Game-Time: Evaluating Temporal Dynamics in Spoken Language Models

abstract: Full-Duplex Speech Language Models (FD-SLMs) enable real-time, overlapping conversational interactions, offering a more dynamic user experience compared to traditional half-duplex models. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on evaluating single-round interactions, neglecting the complexities of multi-round communication. Evaluating FD-SLMs in multi-round settings poses significant challenges, including blurred turn boundaries in communication and context inconsistency during model inference. Also, existing benchmarks often focus solely on evaluating conversational features, neglecting other critical aspects. To address these gaps, we introduce MTR-DuplexBench, a novel benchmark designed for a comprehensive multi-round evaluation of FD-SLMs. MTR-DuplexBench not only segments continuous full-duplex dialogues into discrete turns for turn-by-turn assessment but also incorporates various evaluation aspects, including conversational features, dialogue quality, instruction following, and safety. Experimental results reveal that current FD-SLMs face difficulties in maintaining consistent performance across multiple rounds and evaluation dimensions, highlighting the necessity and effectiveness of our benchmark. The benchmark and code will be available in the future.
title: MTR-DuplexBench: Towards a Comprehensive Evaluation of Multi-Round Conversations for Full-Duplex Speech Language Models

abstract:
We introduce AudioBench, a universal benchmark designed to evaluate Audio
Large Language Models (AudioLLMs). It encompasses 8 distinct tasks and 26
datasets, among which, 7 are newly proposed datasets. The evaluation targets
three main aspects: speech understanding, audio scene understanding, and voice
understanding (paralinguistic). Despite recent advancements, there lacks a
comprehensive benchmark for AudioLLMs on instruction following capabilities
conditioned on audio signals. AudioBench addresses this gap by setting up
datasets as well as desired evaluation metrics. Besides, we also evaluated the
capabilities of five popular models and found that no single model excels
consistently across all tasks. We outline the research outlook for AudioLLMs
and anticipate that our open-sourced evaluation toolkit, data, and leaderboard
will offer a robust testbed for future model developments.

organization_info.name: Centre for Frontier AI Research (CFAR), A*STAR

keyInsights: AudioBench establishes a standardized framework for consistent comparison and progress tracking, thereby accelerating AudioLLM development.
The benchmark's findings highlight critical research directions, such as improving long-form audio processing and enhancing generalized instruction following in AudioLLMs.
The validation of an open-source "model-as-judge" makes advanced evaluation techniques for free-form responses more accessible and reproducible for the research community.
originalProblem: A lack of a comprehensive and standardized benchmark existed for evaluating Audio Large Language Models (AudioLLMs).
Existing AudioLLM evaluations used disparate datasets, limited task scopes, and inconsistent metrics, hindering systematic comparison across models.
There was an absence of suitable evaluation methodologies for assessing open-ended text generation from audio inputs in AudioLLMs.
results: No single evaluated AudioLLM consistently outperforms others across all 8 tasks and 26 datasets on AudioBench.
AudioLLMs generally struggle with long-form ASR and speech-intensive tasks (SQA, SI), where a Whisper+Llama3 cascade model often demonstrates superior performance.
AudioLLMs show strengths in paralinguistic features (emotion, accent, gender recognition) and non-verbal audio scene understanding tasks, outperforming cascade models.
Model performance exhibits sensitivity to training exposure and prompt diversity, indicating ongoing challenges in instruction-following generalization.
solution: Introduces AudioBench, a universal benchmark encompassing 8 distinct tasks across speech understanding, audio scene understanding, and voice understanding.
Curates 26 datasets, including 7 newly proposed or adapted ones, totaling over 400 hours of audio and more than 100,000 samples.

Validates LLaMA-3-70B-Instruct as a robust, open-source "model-as-judge" (showing >0.85 Spearman's correlation with GPT-4) for evaluating open-ended generation, alongside standard metrics like WER and METEOR.
paper_summary.summary: A universal benchmark for Audio Large Language Models (AudioLLMs), named AudioBench, is introduced, comprising 8 tasks, 26 datasets, and robust evaluation metrics to assess instruction-following capabilities.
